title: Different market structures
paperName: Cambridge International A Level Economics (P3 & P4)
summary: 9708 A Level market structures — perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, and monopolistic competition with GeoGebra.
learningObjectives: Compare features of major market structures.
Draw firm demand and MR curves under monopoly.
Explain short-run and long-run equilibrium in competitive markets.
Evaluate efficiency implications of different structures.

simpleExplanation.title: How market structure affects price
simpleExplanation.summary: Perfect competitors are price takers with horizontal demand. Monopolists face downward sloping demand and MR below AR.
steps: Perfect competition: P = MC in long run, productive efficiency.
Monopoly: P > MC — allocative inefficiency possible.
Oligopoly: kinked demand, collusion, game theory.
Monopolistic competition: differentiation, excess capacity long run.

flashcards.back: Many firms, homogeneous product, free entry/exit, perfect information, price taker (horizontal demand for individual firm).
P = MC = min AC — normal profit only (AR tangent to AC). Productive and allocative efficiency achieved.

flashcards.front: Why is MR below AR for a monopolist?
flashcards.back: To sell more, the monopolist must lower price on all units. MR = P + Q(ΔP/ΔQ); for linear demand, MR has twice the slope of AR.
Monopolist produces where MC = MR with P > MC — underproduces relative to social optimum → allocative inefficiency (DWL).
Assumes rivals match price cuts but not price rises → demand more elastic above current price, less elastic below → price rigidity.
Free entry erodes supernormal profit → tangency of D and AC with D downward sloping → excess capacity (output < min AC).

sections.content: **Market structures** (7.6) often compare **monopoly vs perfect competition** in one essay. Draw **MR below D** for monopoly every time. Builds on **7.5 costs and revenue**.

The individual firm faces a **horizontal demand curve** at market price (P = MR = AR). It is a **price taker**.

**Short run:** firm may earn supernormal profit (AR > AC), normal profit (AR = AC), or loss (AR < AC).

**Long run:** free entry/exit drives profit to **normal** — AR = AC at minimum AC. Also P = MC → **allocative and productive efficiency**.

A **monopolist** faces the **market demand curve** (downward sloping). **MR lies below AR** because lowering price to sell more reduces revenue on inframarginal units.

Profit max: **MC = MR**. Price is read from the demand curve at this output — **P > MC**.

**Allocative inefficiency:** output is less than the competitive level. **DWL** exists. However, monopolist may achieve **productive efficiency** if it produces at min AC (natural monopoly argument).

items: **Oligopoly** — few dominant firms, barriers to entry, mutual interdependence. Models: kinked demand, game theory (prisoner's dilemma), collusion (cartel).
**Monopolistic competition** — many firms, differentiated products, free entry. Short-run supernormal profit; long-run tangency → normal profit with **excess capacity**.
**Contestable markets** — threat of entry disciplines incumbent even with one firm (no sunk costs).

sections.question:
A monopolist faces P = 50 − Q (linear demand) and TC = 100 + 10Q.

(a) Derive the MR function.
(b) Find profit-maximising Q and P.
(c) Compare with the perfectly competitive outcome.
sections.solution:
(a) **TR = P × Q = (50 − Q)Q = 50Q − Q²**
**MR = dTR/dQ = 50 − 2Q**

(For linear demand P = a − bQ, MR = a − 2bQ.)

(b) **MC = dTC/dQ = 10**
Profit max: MC = MR → 10 = 50 − 2Q → Q = **20**
**P = 50 − 20 = £30**

**Profit** = TR − TC = (30 × 20) − (100 + 200) = 600 − 300 = **£300**

(c) **Perfect competition:** P = MC → 50 − Q = 10 → Q = **40**, P = **£10**

Monopoly produces **half the competitive output** at **three times the price**.

**DWL:** welfare loss from underproduction — consumers pay more and buy less. Monopolist gains producer surplus but total welfare falls.

sections.content: Comparison essays need **two diagrams side by side**: PC (horizontal D, P = min AC) vs monopoly (downward D, MR below AR, P > MC). State efficiency implications explicitly for each structure.

items.label: 7.5 Types of cost, revenue and profit
7.8 Differing objectives and policies of firms

steps.caption: Perfect competition: many firms, homogeneous product, price taker.
steps.embedHint: Horizontal demand curve for individual firm.

steps.caption: Monopoly: single seller — downward sloping demand, barriers to entry.
steps.embedHint: MR below AR for linear demand.

steps.caption: Oligopoly: interdependence — kinked demand, game theory.
Monopolistic competition: differentiated products, free entry long run.
steps.embedHint: Excess capacity at tangency of D and AC.
